coredata.dc:title: Effect of surface grafting with quaternized carbon quantum dots on nanofiltration membrane removing contaminants from micro-polluted river water
coredata.dc:creator: Song, Yuefei
Wang, Yongxin
Hou, Mengxiang
Du, Chenghao
Chen, Kexin
Zhang, Yanzhuo
Jiang, Kai
coredata.dc:description: It is highly encouraged to efficiently and stably remove heavy metals (HMs) and pharmaceutical active compounds (PhACs) from practical micro-polluted river water by one-stage process based on novel customized nanomaterials. For this purpose, the effect of grafting 0D quaternized carbon quantum dots (QCDs) on the resultant nanofiltration membrane microstructure, macroscopic removal performance and associated environmental risk reduction features was explored detailly, compared with the unmodified, doping-modified and other reported counterparts. The results showed that membrane skin characteristics including wettability, smoothness and positive charge could be better optimized through surface grafting (with water contact angle, root mean square roughness and isoelectric point of 39.4o, 7.39 ± 0.52 nm and 8.6, respectively) than embedding modification (46.5o, 9.10 ± 0.48 nm and 8.1) by the tailored 0D QCDs. Consequently, the optimal QCDs-grafted nanofiltration membrane exhibited excellent antiadhesion (R ir = 1.39%), satisfactory rejection (all above 98.5% for target HMs and PhACs) and relatively higher water permeability (20.8 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1) in a 40-hour test. Also, it was capable of reducing raw river water with medium/low pollution level to a non-polluting state. More interestingly, it was found that rejections for target micropollutants in practical water environment were always slightly higher than those in ideal water environment. In brief, this study convincingly demonstrated that nanocomposite membranes prepared by QCDs grafting rather than doping modification was more suitable for reliably and stably removing a range of specific small-sized contaminants in practical wastewater scenarios.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quaternized carbon quantum dots
Nanofiltration membrane
Pharmaceutically active compounds
Heavy metals
Environmental risk reduction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of QCDs-grafted TFC membrane and its counterparts
Performance of QCDs-grafted TFC membrane and its counterparts
Performance comparison with other related studies

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: State Key Laboratory of Water Resource Protection and Utilization in Coal Mining, China
Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province, China (No. 222300420475 ), Open Fund of State Key Laboratory of Water Resource Protection and Utilization in Coal Mining, China ( GJNY-18–73.14 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characteristics of the resultant membranes: FTIR (a) and XPS (b) spectra, zeta potentials and wettabilities (c), FE-SEM (d, e) and AFM (f, g) surface micrographs.
Pore size distribution and porosity (a) and cross-section images (b, c) of both neat and QCDs grafted TFC membranes.

3DEEM fluorescence spectra of the relevant influent/effluent samples: raw surface water (a), UF filtrate in the absence (b) and presence (c) of mixed PhACs, NF permeate for QCDs-TFC (d) and TFC (e) membranes as well as influent matrices only containing selected PhACs (f).

Perm-selectivity properties of QCDs-grafted TFC membrane and its counterparts tested with 1000 μg·L−1 target PhACs and HMs in simulated (in the absence (a, c) and presence (e) of multiple influent matrices) and real (b, d) micro-polluted feedwater samples.
Flux evolution (a), fouling resistance (b) of QCDs-grafted TFC membrane and its counterparts tested with the real micro-polluted feedwater.
Water CAs and CLSM scanning pictures as well as related fluorescence intensity profiles of the fouled and rinsed TFC (a, b, c) and QCDs-TFC (d, e, f) membranes at the end of the fouling experiment.

Comparison of the separation (a) and anti-fouling (b) performance of QCDs-TFC membrane with other recently reported NF membranes [13,14,21,37–55]. The specific literatures corresponding to these involved membranes were listed in Table S5.
RQ values for target PhACs (a), WQI and Pn values for the six selected HMs (b) in QCDs-TFC permeate and its counterparts in practical water environment.

head.ce:title: Effect of surface grafting with quaternized carbon quantum dots on nanofiltration membrane removing contaminants from micro-polluted river water

ce:author.ce:contributor-role: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing − review & editing
Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Visualization, Validation, Writing − original draft

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Yellow River and Huai River Water Environmental and Pollution Control, Ministry of Education, School of Environment, Henan Normal University, 46 East of Construction Road, Xinxiang 453007, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Yellow River and Huai River Water Environmental and Pollution Control, Ministry of Education, School of Environment, Henan Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Yellow River and Huai River Water Environmental and Pollution Control, Ministry of Education, School of Environment, Henan Normal University, 46 East of Construction Road, Xinxiang, 453007, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is highly encouraged to efficiently and stably remove heavy metals (HMs) and pharmaceutical active compounds (PhACs) from practical micro-polluted river water by one-stage process based on novel customized nanomaterials. For this purpose, the effect of grafting 0D quaternized carbon quantum dots (QCDs) on the resultant nanofiltration membrane microstructure, macroscopic removal performance and associated environmental risk reduction features was explored detailly, compared with the unmodified, doping-modified and other reported counterparts. The results showed that membrane skin characteristics including wettability, smoothness and positive charge could be better optimized through surface grafting (with water contact angle, root mean square roughness and isoelectric point of 39.4o, 7.39 ± 0.52 nm and 8.6, respectively) than embedding modification (46.5o, 9.10 ± 0.48 nm and 8.1) by the tailored 0D QCDs. Consequently, the optimal QCDs-grafted nanofiltration membrane exhibited excellent antiadhesion (R ir = 1.39%), satisfactory rejection (all above 98.5% for target HMs and PhACs) and relatively higher water permeability (20.8 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1) in a 40-hour test. Also, it was capable of reducing raw river water with medium/low pollution level to a non-polluting state. More interestingly, it was found that rejections for target micropollutants in practical water environment were always slightly higher than those in ideal water environment. In brief, this study convincingly demonstrated that nanocomposite membranes prepared by QCDs grafting rather than doping modification was more suitable for reliably and stably removing a range of specific small-sized contaminants in practical wastewater scenarios.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Surface runoff water, especially in the section flowing through industrial urban areas, usually contains ubiquitous trace-level contaminants such as heavy metals (HMs) and emerging pharmaceutical active compounds (PhACs). These micropollutants might further cause potential harm to downstream ecosystems and human health through bioaccumulation and biomagnification in the food chain [1–3]. However, traditional water treatment processes such as activated sludge and flocculation settling are difficult to effectively prevent and control these increasingly troubling water environmental issues [4]. In contrast, some flourishing nanotechnologies such as adsorption, photocatalysis and permeable membrane processes have been demonstrated to possess potential application prospects as advanced treatment methods [5,6]. Thereinto, it is worth noting that nanofiltration (NF) membrane, based on sub-nanoscale tunable pore size and surface charge, has exhibited relatively good removal performance in such application scenarios [7]. Generally, the existing relevant reports focused primarily on elucidating the separation mechanisms of various PhACs and HMs in a range of simulated/real micro-polluted waters, including molecular sieving, Donnan exclusion, partitioning and adsorption effects for virgin membranes, and extra cake enhanced concentration polarization (CECP) and secondary barrier for fouled ones, respectively [8,9]. Furthermore, the influence effects of some pivotal elements including the physico-chemical features of membranes, target pollutants, feedwater and operating parameters were also discussed in depth [10].

Nevertheless, the known reports were largely limited to the following aspects. Firstly, although lots of mainstream studies were devoted to breaking the long-standing perm-selectivity upper bound by customizing various styles of membrane materials and clarifying the corresponding separation mechanisms, relatively less follow-up researches on anti-fouling NF membranes had been reported together [7,11]. Especially considering the other cumbersome and costly redundant steps (including optimizing cleaning protocols, pretreatment and operating parameters, etc.), fabricating NF membrane with anti-adhesion capacity could spontaneously suppress fouling from the viewpoint of molecular interactions and the associated negative effects [12]. Secondly, the vast majority of efforts only referred commercial/homemade negatively charged polyamide NF membranes, which inevitably sacrificed the separation performance for positively-charged PhACs, let alone the inherent multivalent cationic HMs [13,14]. Thirdly, those small-sized PhACs (molecular weight ≤350 Da) with weak steric hindrances were hard to be reliably rejected by commercial NF filter elements (removal rate ≤85%), while the dense NF membranes (molecular weight cut-offs between 150 and 350 Da) constructed in response showed relatively low permeabilities [15]. Finally, the application scenarios were more limited to simulated wastewater, in which background matrices usually only involved no/simple traditional model pollutants (such as the rich contained Ca2+/Mg2+ ion, humic acid, sodium alginate, bovine serum protein or SiO2 colloid, etc.) [16]. But in fact, the interactions derived from the complex background matrices contained in real micro-polluted surface river water were difficult to be fully reflected in its counterparts, such as colloidal particles (<0.45 µm), endogenous dissolved metabolites and other trace level components [17,18]. Therefore, it is undoubtedly worthy of encouragement to construct a new type of positively charged dense NF membrane with both excellent perm-selectivity and anti-adhesion property and carry out its research on the efficient and stable removal of HMs and PhACs in real surface micro-polluted water.

In general, the well-reported thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membrane flux was always significantly improved in comparison with the unmodified one [7]. This may be mainly benefited from the internal more regular water production channels rooted from various incorporating hydrophilic nanoparticles (such as graphene oxide, metal organic frameworks, covalent organic frameworks, β-cyclodextrin and carbon quantum dots, etc.), induced matrix defect and improved surface hydrophilicity [9]. However, its retention was only moderately improved or even compromised due to inevitable nanoparticles agglomeration and defect formation within the matrix [19]. Of course, by selecting or designing nanoparticles reasonably, the microstructure and macroscopic performance of TFN membrane could also be regulated finely or even in the exact opposite direction. For instance, Sun et al. surprisedly found that the introduction of sulfonated carbon quantum dots further endowed the TFN-SCD membrane with a looser polyamide structure and a more negative surface charge, probably because of water molecules surrounding –SO3H group preventing the firm fixing of polyamide and the anionic groups on the doped nanomaterials [20]. In contrast, in previous work, we found that cationized quaternized carbon quantum dots (QCDs) could positively participate in instead of retarding the subsequent interfacial polymerization reaction during the doping modification process, thereby fabricating a dense NF membrane with a significantly increased isoelectric point (IEP) [21]. Furthermore, it's crucial to note that the aforesaid TFN membranes also generally exhibited improved anti-adhesion properties to varying degrees in the standard simulated fouling test processes. While for real water body with a stronger fouling propensity, such improvements in anti-fouling performance would undoubtedly be discounted and questionable [5]. However, when 0D QCDs are grafted onto the resultant NF membrane surface instead of doping into its matrix, the room for enhancing membrane surface smoothness and wettability ought to be more promising [7]. And the accompanying uniform coverage of QCDs nanoparticles on the prepared NF membrane surface will also increase its positive charge to a greater extent [8]. These two factors are obviously beneficial to reinforce its fouling resistance and selectivity towards cationic small-sized micropollutants, respectively. In addition, it would be highly desirable to quantitatively assess the positive and negative effects brought about by this modification on permeability.

To this end, in this study, we firstly fabricated a thin-film composite (TFC) membrane by anchoring the cationized 0D QCDs onto polyethylenimine (PEI)-based polyamide membrane through surface graft modification. Then, taking the unmodified membrane and the QCDs-TFN one fabricated by doping modification as counterparts, the effects of different QCDs incorporating techniques on both skin features and body microstructure of the resultant membranes were comprehensively discussed. Subsequently, the consequent pros and cons for their perm-selectivities and anti-fouling performance during the courses of removing HMs and small-sized PhACs from the practical Weihe river water and the underlying reasons were also deeply compared and explored, respectively. Finally, the associated environmental risk reduction characteristics by the modified permeate membrane were assessed together. This study will provide useful insights for tailoring nanostructured membrane materials designed to efficiently and reliably remove a range of specific small-sized contaminants from real surface micro-polluted water.

2-chloro-1-methylpyridinium iodide (CMPI, >97.0%), PEI (50% solution) with MW of 80 kDa, polyethylene glycols (PEGs) with molecular weights (MWs = 100, 200, 400, 600 and 800 Da), sodium dodecyl sulphate (SDS), n-hexane (99%), trimesoyl chloride (TMC, 98%), silica (Si), E.Coli, highly purified methanol (≥99.9%), commonly used inorganic salts (Na2CO3, NaOH, MgCl2, NaCl, Na2SO4, MgSO4) of analytical purity were all purchased from Sinopharm Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Also, other inorganic electrolytes that represented six HMs involving CrCl3·9 H2O, CdCl2, NiCl2, CuCl2, ZnCl2 and Pb(NO3)2 of the same purity were gained from Deen Reagents Co., Ltd (Zhengzhou, China). Four HPLC-grade PhACs involving trimethoprim (TMP), atenolol (ATN), carbamazepine (CBZ) and sulfamethoxazole (SMX) with significant differences in physicochemical features (see Table S1 for details) and similar small MWs (<300 Da) as well as humic acid (HA) were all obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China) [5]. Both of them were selected as target micropollutants because they were frequently detected in Weihe river in the city of Xinxiang, China [22]. Deionized (DI) water (3.5 μS·cm−1) was employed for all experiments, unless otherwise indicated.

Commercial flat-plate polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) ultrafiltration (UF) membrane with molecular weight cut-off of 60 kDa and pure water flux of 155 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1 was bought from Daona Membrane technology Co., Ltd (Hangzhou, China) and soaked in sequence to 0.15 wt% SDS aqueous solution as well as DI water before use. Self-made hollow fiber UF membrane module with the same material and molecular weight cut-off as well as a surface area of 0.02 m2 was hired for strict pretreatment.

As for simulated NF feedwater, two types of samples were involved. Namely, all selected PhACs or heavy metal salts were spiked and dissolved in Milli-Q water (0.055 μS·cm−1) with the assistance of highly purified methanol if needed, respectively, to achieve a constant concentration of 1000 μg·L−1 for each component. The presence of model colloidal, organic and biological foulants including Si (1 mg·L−1), HA (20 mg·L−1) and E.Coli (30000 CFU·L−1) was also involved. In terms of real micro-polluted influent, raw water sampled from Weihe river in Xinxiang urban section (its critical properties presented the first column in Table S2) was continuously and rapidly settled, then pre-treated by PVDF UF membrane module. Next, each PhAC component was added in accord with the preparation of the simulated influent. Alternatively, the real wastewater was only pretreated by initial settling for consideration of more economical process feasibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of QCDs-grafted TFC membrane and its counterparts
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of QCDs-grafted TFC membrane generally comprises two steps, namely polyamide active layer by interfacial polymerization and QCDs nanoparticles grafting. The first step for fabricating nascent TFC membrane could be seen in our previous work [21]. Briefly, PVDF substrate was immersed into PEI aqueous phase (5.0 g·L−1) comprising Na2CO3 (0.8 g·L−1) and SDS (0.5 g·L−1) as an acid acceptance agent and a surfactant for 5 min. Then, the redundant solution on substrate surface was blown away. Subsequently, the PEI-saturated membrane was soaked into the n-hexane oil phase including TMC (1.0 g·L−1) for 3 min. After removing the unreacted TMC by rinsing with original n-hexane, the control membrane named TFC membrane dried in air. As for the second procedure, a one-step CMPI grafting method was applied to graft 0D QCDs nanoparticles on membrane surface accompanied by minimizing the nullification of acyl chloride, thus enabling the grafting efficiency to be improved [23]. To be specific, a new aqueous solution containing CMPI (0.3 g·L−1), NaOH (0.3 g·L−1) and QCDs (0.6 g·L−1) at optimized concentrations (see Fig. S1) was poured onto the TFC membrane for 10 min, allowing for chemical grafting. Wherein, QCDs nanoparticles were prepared in a one-step pyrolysis technique [24] and their morphologies were shown in Fig. S2. In addition, the QCDs-TFN membrane with the same components was employed for more extensive performance comparison stemmed from differences in whether nanoparticles were modified by doping or grafting method. Finally, all the unmodified TFC, QCDs-grafted TFC and QCDs-doped TFN membranes were cured at 70 ℃ for 10 min. The as-prepared membranes were stored in DI water at 4 °C for later use.

A laboratory-scale cross-flow membrane evaluating equipment with an area of 56.6 cm2 was employed to investigate NF separating performance, as schematically illustrated in Fig. S3. Initially, each membrane coupon was compacted at 4.0 bar for 1 h to make the system stable. Then, the formal separation performance test for each batch was carried out at 6 bar under 25 ± 0.3 oC and inlet flow rate of 15.6 L·h−1 lasted for 2 h. Water permeability (L·m−2·h−1·bar−1) was measured indirectly by weighing the mass of the permeate over a designated time interval. Rejection (%) of single type of micropollutant was gained by calculating the percentage of rejected solute content to its concentration in NF influent.

Hereafter, three-cycle fouling and associated physical rinsing tests for the modified QCDs-TFC membrane and its counterparts were performed for 120 h under the identical original operational conditions as the separation performance. In this circumstance, only the real micro-polluted influent was employed because of its relatively strong fouling potential. At the outset, the initial pure water permeability (J 0) was achieved by the compacted NF membranes under non-fouling condition. Then, the final permeability (J f) was obtained after filtrating the qualified real micro-polluted feedwater for 12 h. Finally, the fouled membrane element was rinsed promptly with DI water for 0.5 h to wipe off reversible pollutants and the pure water permeability after physical flushing (J p,n, n = 1, 2 and 3) was determined again. Accordingly, the relevant fouling index containing total fouling ratio (R t), flux recovery ratio (FRR), irreversible fouling ratio (R ir) of the as-prepared membranes were measured according to Eqs. (1)–(3) [25]:
(1)
R
t
=
J
0
−
J
p , n
J
0
× 100 %
(2)
FRR =
J
p , n
J
0
× 100 %
(3)
R
ir
=
J
0
−
J
p , n
J
0
× 100 %

Surface and cross-sectional morphologies, wettabilities, roughnesses, surface chemical components and structure of polyamide active layers before and after grafting modification were detected by FE-SEM (SU8010, Hitachi, Japan) with the aid of gold sputter-coating, contact angle analyzer (Theta, Biolin Scientific, Finland) via s sessile drop method with 4 μL droplets of DI water at 25 oC, AFM (Multimode 8, Bruker, America), XPS (ESCALAB250Xi, Thermo Fisher, America) and ATR-FTIR (Spectrum 400 F, PerkinElmer, America), respectively. Besides, the IEP of each membrane was detected by streaming potential method using a SurPASS electrokinetic analyzer (Anton Paar GmbH, Austria) under 1.0 mol·m−3 KCl solution with feed temperature 25 oC and pH 7.0 as well as operating pressure 180 kPa. Meanwhile, the apparent pore size and its distribution as well as porosity of each resultant membrane were detected by PEG retention experiment and Hagen-Poiseuille equation [26]. Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM, TCS SP8 MP, Germany) was employed to visualize the distribution of proteins on the fouled/rinsed membrane functional layers with the assistance of fluorescence dye following the method developed by Du et al. [27].

Chromaticity, turbidity, total organic carbon (TOC), total dissolved solids (TDS) and pH of raw surface water, NF feedwater/permeate samples were determined by dilution multiple method (GB 11903–89), digital turbidimeter (WZS-186, INESA, China), TOC/TNb analyzer (VarioTOC cube, Elementar, Germany), conductivity meter (DDS-307A, Leici, China) and pH meter (PHSJ-3 F, Leici, China), respectively. Concentrations of target PhACs and HMs were determined by a liquid chromatographic instrument (Alliance E2695, Waters, America) and an inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS, ELAN DRC-e, America), respectively. The three-dimensional excitation-emission matrix (3DEEM) fluorescence spectra of wastewater samples before and after each treatment were measured by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (FP-6500, Jasco, Japan). Emitted fluorescence and excitation were measured from 280 to 600 nm and 200–500 nm at 5 nm steps, respectively.

To assess the environmental risk reduction effect by the as-fabricated NF membranes for the real micro-polluted surface river water, the risk quotient (RQ) of selected PhACs and pollution index of target HMs were calculated, respectively. For the former, RQ of each residual PhAC in NF permeate was calculated through Eq. (4):
(4)
RQ =
MEC ∙ AF
E
C
50
where MEC is the measured environmental content, AF is a safety factor of 1000 and 100 for acute and chronic toxicities and EC
50 is median effect concentration [28].

Besides, water quality index (WQI) and Nemerow pollution index (Pn) were used to evaluate the potential ecological hazards of HMs before and after NF membrane treatment. WQI is a comprehensive representation of different parameters that could influence water availability and quality, which was computed based on Eq. (5):
(5)
WQI =
1
n
∑
i
n
P
i
where n is the number of speciation of HMs, P
i is the single factor pollution index. Thereinto, P i was expressed by Eq. (6):
(6)
P
i
=
C
i
Q
i
where C i/Q i is the measured/reference content of element i, respectively.

Pn was also employed to investigate the status of multiple heavy metal pollutants at sampling sites in Weihe river basin, which was computed by Eq. (7):
(7)
Pn =
max
P
i
2
+ ave
P
i
2
2
General ranking criteria for RQ, WQI and Pn were gathered and summarized in Table S3 [29,30].

The physicochemical properties and morphologies of the resultant membrane skin layers were systematically characterized, as illustrated in Fig. 1. Firstly, it could be seen clearly from ATR-FTIR results in Fig. 1a that four peaks at 2927, 2854, 1562 and 1670 cm−1 were observed in the spectra of both TFC and QCDs-TFC membranes, which correspond to amide (I), amide (II), N–H bending and CO stretching vibrations, respectively, manifesting the successful formation of polyamide active layer. After the FTIR spectrum of QCDs precursor was also included in the comparison, it could be found that QCDs-TFC membrane showed discernibly enhanced peak intensities at 1670 and 3440 cm−1 than the unmodified TFC membrane, which were related to the abundant quaternary ammonium groups carried by the grafted QCDs nanoparticles [31]. Of course, its strong characteristic band at 3440 cm−1 was also benefited from the unavoidable adsorption of water molecules by the increased surface positive charge, as proved by the IEP changes for both resultant membranes (see Fig. 1c). XPS results plotted in Fig. 1b amazingly demonstrated that contribution of N 1 s somewhat reduced after QCDs grafting modification. This is probably due to the successful introduction of quaternized group in QCDs at the expense of moderately increasing itself carbon chain concurrently, so that the former N 1 s signal is actually weaker than that of the bare PEI-based polyamide active layer. In addition, as shown in Fig. 1c, the IEP of resultant NF membrane surface shifted to the right to 8.6, which would greatly facilitate the removal of cationic target pollutants.

As exhibited in Fig. 1(d-g), a typical “ridge-and-valley” morphology was presented for both membranes based on FE-SEM and AFM results, which verified again that typical dense polyamide layers were successfully fabricated on the PVDF substrate. Simultaneously, the protrusions sticking out from the CMPI mediating QCDs-grafted membrane surface were generally less scattered nodular and more flattened in comparison to that from the bare membrane. This's because the small sized and common well-dispersed of 0D QCDs were intertwined with or even coated onto the virgin PEI-amide matrix and gave the modified composite membrane surface more extraordinary smoothness. Consequently, the root mean square roughness (Rms) values were 12.8 ± 0.59 and 7.39 ± 0.52 nm for control TFC and QCDs-TFC membranes, respectively. The related morphological results manifested that QCDs nanoparticles were preferentially grafted in the “valley” area, which is beneficial to mitigate membrane fouling. Notably, the measured static water contact angle (CA) at 10 s decreased evidently from 67.1o for nascent TFC membrane to 39.4o for QCDs-TFC membrane, especially considering that the result was achieved under the adverse trend of a significant decrease in membrane surface roughness. Even compared to the optimal QCDs-TFN membrane with water CA, Rms and IEP of 46.5o, 9.10 ± 0.48 nm and 8.1, respectively [21], all of these powerfully indicate that the tailored 0D QCDs, which have tunable positive zeta potential, good hydrophilicity and uniform spherical dot-like structure, could more effectively improve membrane surface characteristics including wettability, smoothness and charge through surface grafting than embedding modification.

Pore size analysis based on the rejection test for a series of neutral solutes ( Fig. 2a) showed that QCDs-TFC membrane had a slightly smaller nominal pore size, larger porosity (0.436 nm and 31.8%) than those of the unmodified TFC membrane (0.453 nm and 30.3%) without worsening dispersion degree of pore size distribution, which suggest that grafting the skin layer with 0D nanostructured quaternized monomer could help to finely manipulate NF membrane body microstructure, making it possible to increase precise rejection selectivity. Besides, the cross-section morphologies of both membranes were also studied by FE-SEM, as depicted in Fig. 3(b-c). In fact, the functional layer thickness of QCDs-TFC membrane increased only slightly by 4 nm after modification, well below the threshold of concern for reluctantly sacrificing the water permeability in our plan. It's concluded that the above-mentioned post-grafting treatment did not significantly increase the longitudinal hydraulic resistance of the prepared membrane material. This might be closely related to the small size of 0D QCDs and the preferential grafting to the valley region of pristine PEI-amide matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of QCDs-grafted TFC membrane and its counterparts

ce:section.ce:para: To clarify the evolution of categories during the removal of organics in real micro-polluted surface river water by the dual membrane process under the background of solute-solute interaction, the relevant EEM spectrum of the feed/product sample of each treatment unit was characterized, as depicted in Fig. 3. Two strong peaks in the raw surface river water located in regions IV and III relevant to humic-like and fulvic-like substances were observed, respectively [32]. Both peaks were attenuated after UF pretreatment, especially for larger sized humic acids. However, EEM spectrum of UF filtrate in the presence of mixed PhACs displayed in Fig. 3c was discovered to be blue shifted obviously. In specific, two new characteristic peaks extended across regions I and II related to aromatic proteins and sited in region V related to soluble microbial byproduct-like compounds were derived. Considering the background matrixes in real micro-polluted influent (see Table S2) and the fluorescence spectrum of influent matrices only containing selected PhACs (see Fig. 3f), it could be speculated that this was mainly caused by the solute-solute interaction between some PhACs containing protein-like structures with the residual natural/endogenous soluble organics assisted by the bridging effect of cations such as Ca2+, Mg2+ and HMs [33]. It could be seen from Fig. 4(d-e) that the fluorescence response signals of both TFC membrane permeate samples were weakened to almost negligible, except for the corresponding sporadic fulvic/humic acids. This is most likely caused by a few aliphatic hydrophobic natural organics with little steric hindrances [34,35]. Significantly, the fluorescence signal in QCDs-TFC permeate was slightly weaker in this circumstance.

Effect of embedding/grafting 0D QCDs or not on the as-fabricated TFC, QCDs-TFN and QCDs-TFC membranes separation performance is assessed involving both simulated and real micro-polluted feedwater samples, as shown in Fig. 4. Besides, all other indicators of real water after terminal treatment were listed in Table S2. In terms of PhACs rejecting effort, it appeared surprisingly from Fig. 4(a-b) that compared with bare TFC membrane, QCDs-TFN and QCDs-TFC membranes exhibited perfect mirror-symmetric transformation in the order of rejection rate of target PhACs, whether for simulated or real influent. Of course, these changes were achieved under the condition of enlarging the removal rate of each component, especially for QCDs-TFC membrane. For example, in the case of simulated feedwater, the rejection rates against SMX, CBZ, ATN and TMP by QCDs-TFC membrane were 98.5%, 98.9%, 99.5% and 99.8%, respectively, which were all greater than those by QCDs-TFN membrane (98.2%, 98.6%, 99.3% and 99.5%), let alone the unmodified TFC membrane (95.3%, 94.2%, 93.0% and 92.7%). However, it was also found that NF membrane permeability increment caused by grafting modification was obviously lower than that by embedding modification. These may be due to the following trade-offs. On the one hand, QCDs-TFN and QCDs-TFC membranes were indeed cationic modified from neat TFC one with weak negative zeta potential of − 2.93–5.50 and 9.82 mV under neutral test condition (see Fig. 1c), which might bring earth-shaking change in the electrostatic interaction between solute and membrane during NF separation process, especially for the latter. For instance, for the rejection rate of positively charged TMP with a higher density of electron-accepting groups, it appears to be an advantage for the same-charged QCDs-TFC membrane from the viewpoint of electrostatic repulsion, while it shows a disadvantage for the unmodified opposite-charged TFC one. Of course, the relevant rejection rates demonstrates that the electrostatic interaction effect plays a dominant role in the processes of removing target PhACs. Besides, the steric hindrance effect also gradually increased with the narrowing apparent pore sizes of both modified NF membranes (see Fig. 2a) [21]. On the other hand, TFN membrane incorporating 0D QCDs nanoparticles exhibited an enhanced permeate flux than QCDs-TFC membrane, presumably due to the inevitable formation of microstructure defects and the greater porosity in the former thinner polyamide layer with less longitudinal hydraulic resistance [19]. After comparing Fig. 4a and b, it was additionally found that the retention rates of all selected PhACs in real micro-polluted feedwater by both TFC membranes showed a slight but regular increase. This is mainly due to the fact that apart from the freely dissolved state, PhACs in the actual river water also occurred in the forms of large-sized dissolved organic matter (DOM)-associated and particle (<0.45 µm)-associated states under the microscopic solute-solute interaction [17]. Undoubtedly, the latter two species will effectively increase the steric hindrances of target PhACs, thereby raising their observed rejection rates.

Fig. 4(c-d) presents the separating performance of both neat TFC and QCDs-TFC membranes for target HMs under different influent matrices. Perm-selectivity trends followed the aforementioned observations where the permeate flux and removal efficiency for QCDs-TFC membrane were consistently and significantly higher than those for its counterpart, and rejection for each component in practical water environment was always slightly higher than that in ideal water environment, respectively. In these application scenarios, the unmodified TFC membrane had the apparently lowest rejection for Pb2+. This is mainly because, for one thing, due to the smallest hydration radius and fastest diffusion coefficient (see Table S4), it is easy to penetrate through the NF membrane matrix, which makes it difficult to be effectively retained. For the other thing, the electrostatic attraction effect induced by the less negative charged neat TFC membrane also contributes to this result. The retention rates of Cr3+ and Zn2+ were not in a higher order, which may be due to the interference of other relatively plentiful existing forms with reversed charge properties in the feedwater [22]. In brief, the high retention rates all above 98.9% of target HMs and permeabilities greater than 20 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1 both in ideal and practical water environments by QCDs-TFC membrane verified its potential application in the field of advanced treatment of wastewater rich in cationized micropollutants. The effect of foulant composition on target PhACs by virgin and fouled QCDs-TFC membrane at near neutral pH is depicted in Fig. 4e. It was found that the rejection rates of anionic SMX, cationic ATN and TMP slightly decreased and increased, respectively, compared to virgin QCDs-TFC membrane. This is probably mainly because the presence of these different fouling layers shield or even anionic modify the positively-charged membrane surface, which suggests that electrostatic interaction effect becomes the dominant mechanism for ionized PhACs rejection. Besides, the relatively high rejection of SMX might also be attributed to its low adsorption onto membrane surface as well as fouling layers because of its extremely high hydrophilicity (logKow = −0.56, see Table S1) [36]. In contrast, for hydrophobic-nonionized CBZ (pKa = 14.3, logKow = 2.28), the presence of fouling layer seems to act as a CECP role, accelerating its diffusion and penetration through composite membrane.

Effect of embedding/grafting 0D QCDs or not on the as-prepared TFC, QCDs-TFN and QCDs-TFC membranes antifouling performance was also systematically evaluated. Thereinto, time-dependent flux evolution and the associated fouling indexes after the physical cleaning tested with the real micro-polluted influent are shown in Fig. 5. It was found that after running for14h, the permeate fluxes of TFC, QCDs-TFN and QCDs-TFC membranes decreased by 9.8%, 6.9%, and 4.9%, respectively. Subsequently, after physical cleaning with DI water for 30 min, fluxes of these three NF membranes were recovered to 95.6%, 97.9% and 99.5%, respectively, compared to their respective initial values. After repeating this fouling-physical flushing process three times, the final R t and R ir decreased dramatically from 22.3% and 15.0% of TFC membrane to 13.4% and 6.80%, 7.52% and 1.39% of both QCDs-TFN and QCDs-TFC membranes, respectively. The results proved that, compared with the unmodified TFC or even QCDs-TFN membrane through embedding modification, QCDs-TFC membrane exhibited outstanding fouling resistance and antiadhesion in the advanced treatment of micro-polluted river water, especially featured in a very minor irreversible fouling rate. This is mainly because the graft-modified membrane surface is loaded with a large number of evenly distributed hydrophilic 0D QCDs nanoparticles, which greatly optimizes its wettability and smoothness (see Fig. 1) [20], enabling the resulting improvement in membrane anti-fouling performance far better than that by doping modification.

Water CA and CLSM were employed to further characterize the fouling resistance of both membranes during the final batch of fouling-physical flushing process in practical water environment. For this study, CLSM images could precisely represent the spatial distribution of target PhACs with protein-like structure mixed in the foulant layers. Specifically, the results depicted in Fig. 6 showed that compared with the unmodified one, the fouled/rinsed QCDs-TFC membrane surface displayed weaker fluorescence intensity and lower fouling layer thickness. What's more delightful was that the same physical flushing step made the thickness of the fouling layer onto QCDs-grafted TFC membrane surface fell even more on the basis of a lower base. This powerful and highly consistent evidence with the macroscopic fouling resistance index again suggests that QCDs-TFC membrane has an excellent antifouling potential and also just prefers to induce less reversible fouling. During this period, CAs of both fouled TFC and QCDs-TFC membranes increased from 67.1o and 39.4o in their pristine state (see Fig. 1c) to 86.7o and 69.3o, then decreased to 75.6o and 56.4o after physical flushing, respectively. It should be noted from water CA data that the hydrophilicity of QCDs-grafted TFC membrane surface is significantly better than that of the unmodified TFC membrane at any corresponding stage. For instance, the degree of wettability improvement reflected in the decrement or decrease rate of CA associated with the physical flushing process continues this trend. These changes also indirectly prove the antiadhesion surface of QCDs-TFC membrane is indeed modified.

XPS, FTIR, SEM have also been carried out for the membrane post filtration, as depicted in Fig. S4. It could be seen clearly from Fig. S4a that when running to 40 h, contributions of C 1 s and O 1 s increased while N 1 s decreased by 8.26% due to the coverage of CQDs-TFC membrane surface by organic fouling layer. ATR-FTIR spectrum (see Fig. S4b) further confirmed this speculation. It is mainly manifested in the markedly enhanced intensities of one broad peak located at 3250–3745 cm-1 (O−H and N − H bonds) and another characteristic peak sited at 1663 cm-1 (CO bond). This indicated that a large proportion of these foulant ingredients were hydrophilic organics rich in carboxyl and hydroxyl functional groups. Besides, an obvious and relatively dense cake layer with holes formed on the membrane surface (Fig. S4c) when fed with real wastewater. In brief, these characterizations further strongly echo the interpretation of solute-membrane interactions between target PhACs and the optimal CQDs-TFC one summarized in Section 3.2.2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance comparison with other related studies
ce:section.ce:para: Additional separation and fouling experimental results for the real wastewater was only pretreated by initial settling could be seen in Fig. S5. It shows that the lack of effective interception of colloids and macromolecular organics by UF unit induces more solute-solute interactions for target micropollutants, thereby increasing their apparent steric hindrance and rejection rates by terminal NF membrane in a short service time. Meanwhile, the relatively high fouling load causes QCD-TFC membrane permeate flux attenuation to become more severe during the long-term operation. Also, a series of data on the removals of HMs and PhACs from micro-polluted surface waters involving recently reported or commercial NF membranes were collected detailly and compared with the optimal QCDs-TFC membrane, as shown in Fig. 7. The comparison was performed on the basis of perm-selectivity and antifouling performance. As observed, compared with its counterparts, the as-prepared QCDs-grafted TFC membrane exhibited excellent fouling resistance, rather good rejection and relatively higher permeability aiming for HMs and small-sized PhACs removals. Of course, due to its own innate structure mainly manifested in an extra outer grafting layer, the permeate flux of QCDs-TFC membrane is still inferior to those of some conceptual or TFN membranes under standard testing conditions, but showed improved removal efficiency and anti-fouling performance in practical micro-polluted water purification scenarios, especially when coupled with an inexpensive physical flushing step.

The calculated RQs of the four selected PhACs in both resultant NF membrane permeate samples based on their respective EC 50 values in practical water environment are shown in Fig. 8a. Since the RQs of target PhACs were all less than 0.1, it indicated that both NF permeate streams were harmless to aquatic organisms. Specifically, the RQ values in NF permeate rejected by QCDs-TFC membrane were all significantly lower than those rejected by the unmodified TFC membrane. For instance, the former RQ value of TMP with the same-charged property and relatively large steric hindrance was only 1.36 × 10−5, and the corresponding values of the other two charged PhACs (ATN and SMX) were also less than 0.004. Similarly, as presented in Fig. 8b, based on the measured concentration data of HMs in raw river water located in Xinxiang urban area and both TFC membrane permeate samples, WQI and Pn were calculated and used to assess the related potential ecological hazards reduction effect, to provide a powerful intervention measure for prevention and control of surface water pollution. Combining with Table S3, it was found that the actual raw river water was in a medium/low pollution standard level when expressed as WQI and Pn, respectively. In contrast, both NF permeate samples were in a non-polluting state, and as always, the pollution risk values of HMs in QCDs-TFC membrane permeate were obviously lower. All these data strongly suggest that the resultant 0D QCDs grafted NF membrane could be used as an efficient and reliable tool to prevent further migration or potential enrichment of HMs and small-sized PhACs in surface water environment.

Based on the grafting/doping modification or not by hydrophilic cationized 0D QCDs nanoparticles, this study systematically explored its effect on the microstructure of PEI-based polyamide membranes and the macroscopic removal performance of target micropollutants in real river water. The results demonstrated that the resultant NF membranes skin features including wettability, smoothness and positive charge could be better optimized through surface grafting than embedding modification. Specifically, water CA, Rms and IEP of the optimal QCDs-TFC membrane were 39.4o, 7.39 ± 0.52 nm and 8.6, respectively, which were all superior than those of QCDs-TFN membrane (46.5o, 9.10 ± 0.48 nm and 8.1), let alone the unmodified TFC membrane (67.1o, 12.8 ± 0.59 nm and 6.5). In contrast, in terms of its body microstructure, QCDs-TFC membrane only showed a slightly narrowed apparent pore size and larger porosity (0.436 nm and 31.8%) than those of the unmodified one (0.453 nm and 30.3%) without worsening dispersion degree of pore size distribution and longitudinal hydraulic resistance. Consequently, the optimal QCDs-grafted NF membrane exhibited excellent antiadhesion (R ir = 1.39%), satisfactory rejection (all above 98.5% for target HMs and PhACs) and relatively higher water permeability (20.8 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1) during three consecutive batches of fouling-physical rinsing processes. Also, it was capable of reducing raw river water with medium/low pollution level to a non-polluting state. Furthermore, it was found that rejections for target micropollutants in practical water environment were always slightly higher than those in ideal water environment, presumably due to the enhanced steric hindrance derived from DOM-associated and particle (<0.45 µm)-associated states under the microscopic solute-solute interaction. In conclusion, the customized QCDs-grafted membrane might be employed as a reliable and stable barrier to efficiently hinder further migration or diffusion of target micropollutants in practical wastewater scenarios.

Yuefei Song: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing − review & editing. Yongxin Wang: Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Visualization, Validation, Writing − original draft. Mengxiang Hou: Investigation. Chenghao Du: Data curation. Kexin Chen: Validation. Yanzhuo Zhang: Data curation. Kai Jiang: Supervision, Writing − review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province, China (No. 222300420475), Open Fund of State Key Laboratory of Water Resource Protection and Utilization in Coal Mining, China (GJNY-18–73.14).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2022.109244.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effects of organic, biological and colloidal fouling on the removal of pharmaceuticals and personal care products by nanofiltration and reverse osmosis membranes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Lin, Y.L., Effects of organic, biological and colloidal fouling on the removal of pharmaceuticals and personal care products by nanofiltration and reverse osmosis membranes. J. Membr. Sci. 542 (2017) 342–351.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: How to fabricate a negatively charged NF membrane for heavy metal removal via the interfacial polymerization between PIP and TMC?

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Tian, J., Chang, H., Gao, S., et al., 2020. How to fabricate a negatively charged NF membrane for heavy metal removal via the interfacial polymerization between PIP and TMC? Desalination 491, 114499.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanofiltration membranes consisting of quaternized polyelectrolyte complex nanoparticles for heavy metal removal

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ye, C.C., An, Q.F., Wu, J.K., et al., Nanofiltration membranes consisting of quaternized polyelectrolyte complex nanoparticles for heavy metal removal. Chem. Eng. J. 359 (2019) 994–1005.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Interfacial polymerization nanofiltration membrane with visible light photocatalytic self-cleaning performance by incorporation of CQD/TiO2

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Mi, Y., Wang, N., Fang, X., et al., 2021. Interfacial polymerization nanofiltration membrane with visible light photocatalytic self-cleaning performance by incorporation of CQD/TiO2. Sep. Purif. Technol. 277, 119500.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Anti-organic fouling and anti-biofouling poly(piperazineamide) thin film nanocomposite membranes for low pressure removal of heavy metal ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Bera, A., Trivedi, J.S., Kumar, S.B., et al., Anti-organic fouling and anti-biofouling poly(piperazineamide) thin film nanocomposite membranes for low pressure removal of heavy metal ions. J. Hazard. Mater. 343 (2018) 86–97.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Finely tailored pore structure of polyamide nanofiltration membranes for highly-efficient application in water treatment

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Cheng, X., Qin, Y., Ye, Y., et al., 2021. Finely tailored pore structure of polyamide nanofiltration membranes for highly-efficient application in water treatment. Chem. Eng. J. 417, 127976.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel photocatalytic self-cleaning PES nanofiltration membrane incorporating triple metal-nonmetal doped TiO2 (K-B-N-TiO2) for post treatment of biologically treated palm oil mill effluent

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zangeneh, H., Zinatizadeh, A.A., Zinadini, S., et al., A novel photocatalytic self-cleaning PES nanofiltration membrane incorporating triple metal-nonmetal doped TiO2 (K-B-N-TiO2) for post treatment of biologically treated palm oil mill effluent. React. Funct. Polym. 127 (2018) 139–152.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: PMO synthesized and functionalized by p-phenylenediamine as new nanofiller in PES-nanofiltration membrane matrix for efficient treatment of organic dye, heavy metal, and salts from wastewater

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Mehrjo, F., Pourkhabbaz, A., Shahbazi, A., 2021. PMO synthesized and functionalized by p-phenylenediamine as new nanofiller in PES-nanofiltration membrane matrix for efficient treatment of organic dye, heavy metal, and salts from wastewater. Chemosphere 263, 128088.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Biocompatible Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2 nanocomposite as a green nanofiller embedded in PES-nanofiltration membrane matrix for salts, heavy metal ion and dye removal: Long-term operation and reusability tests

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Kamari, S., Shahbazi, A., 2020. Biocompatible Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2 nanocomposite as a green nanofiller embedded in PES-nanofiltration membrane matrix for salts, heavy metal ion and dye removal: Long-term operation and reusability tests. Chemosphere 243, 125282.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Removal of chromium (VI) ions from aqueous solutions using amine-impregnated TiO2 nanoparticles modified cellulose acetate membranes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Gebru, K.A., Das, C., Removal of chromium (VI) ions from aqueous solutions using amine-impregnated TiO2 nanoparticles modified cellulose acetate membranes. Chemosphere 191 (2018) 673–684.
